blocks.content: Functions model relationships where every input has exactly one output. This unit reviews the features you need before studying advanced function families.

blocks.title: Domain and range
blocks.content: The domain is every allowed input. The range is every output the function can produce.

blocks.title: Reading a transformation
blocks.content: For g(x) = -2f(x - 3) + 1, move the graph right 3, reflect it in the x-axis, stretch vertically by 2, then move it up 1.

blocks.title: Inside versus outside
blocks.content: A change inside f( ) affects x-values and moves horizontally in the opposite-looking direction. A change outside affects y-values directly.
An inverse undoes a function. A function has an inverse function only when it is one-to-one on the chosen domain. Composition means feeding the output of one function into another.

blocks.title: Try it
blocks.content:
1. Describe the transformations from f(x) to -3f(x + 2) - 4.
2. Explain why f(x)=x² needs a restricted domain before it has an inverse function.
3. If f(x)=2x+1 and g(x)=x², find (g∘f)(x).

blocks.title: Check your work
blocks.content:
1. Left 2, reflect in x-axis, vertical stretch by 3, down 4.
2. x² is not one-to-one over all real numbers.
3. (2x+1)².

cards.front: What does an inverse function do?
cards.back: It reverses the input-output relationship of the original function.

cards.front: What condition allows a function to have an inverse function?
cards.back: It must be one-to-one on its domain.

questions.question: Which transformation is represented by f(x-4)?
questions.explanation: A horizontal shift inside the function uses the opposite sign.

questions.question: Explain why function composition can be useful in a real-world model.
questions.sample_answer: A strong answer explains that multi-step processes can be represented by applying one relationship after another and gives a relevant example.
